title: Binary Trie min XOR
description: Find minimum XOR pair using Trie

approaches.name: Brute Force O(n²)
approaches.summary: Iterate through all pairs of numbers in the array, compute XOR for each pair, and track the minimum value. This is straightforward but inefficient for large inputs.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Brute Force Minimum XOR Pair
ladders.desc: Find the minimum XOR value among all possible pairs in an array using brute force comparison.
ladders.explanation: The brute force approach to finding the minimum XOR pair is conceptually simple but computationally expensive. The intuition is that to guarantee finding the absolute minimum XOR value, we must examine every possible pair of numbers in the array. This is necessary because XOR is not monotonic—two numbers that are close in value don't necessarily produce a small XOR (e.g., 3 (011) and 4 (100) have XOR 7, which is large despite being consecutive numbers). The algorithm works by initializing a minimum variable to a large value (like infinity), then using nested loops to iterate through all index pairs (i, j) where i < j to avoid duplicate comparisons. For each pair, compute XOR = arr[i] ^ arr[j], and update the minimum if this XOR is smaller. After checking all pairs, return the minimum value. Time complexity is O(n²) because we have n(n-1)/2 pairs to check. Space complexity is O(1) as we only use a few variables. This connects to the parent problem of finding minimum XOR pairs as the most straightforward implementation. Common mistakes include: comparing i and j without ensuring i < j (leading to duplicate pairs and XOR with itself, which is always 0), not initializing min to a sufficiently large value, and misunderstanding that XOR of a number with itself is 0 (which is only relevant if array has duplicates). Real code pattern: def min_xor_brute(arr): min_val = float('inf'); n = len(arr); for i in range(n): for j in range(i+1, n): xor_val = arr[i] ^ arr[j]; if xor_val < min_val: min_val = xor_val; return min_val.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Pairwise XOR Computation Loop
ladders.desc: Implement the nested loop structure that generates all unique pairs and computes their XOR values.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine forms the computational core of the brute force approach. The intuition is that to compare all possible pairs without repetition, we need a systematic way to generate index combinations where the first index is always less than the second. This ensures each pair is considered exactly once. The algorithm uses two nested loops: an outer loop with index i from 0 to n-2, and an inner loop with index j from i+1 to n-1. For each (i, j) pair, we compute XOR = arr[i] ^ arr[j]. This structure guarantees we examine all n(n-1)/2 unique pairs efficiently in code. Time complexity for this sub-routine alone is O(n²) as it performs constant work for each of the quadratic number of pairs. Space complexity is O(1). This feeds into the full problem by providing the mechanism to access all possible pairs for XOR computation. Common mistakes include: using j starting from 0 instead of i+1 (which causes duplicate pairs and self-pairs), forgetting to handle empty or single-element arrays, and off-by-one errors in loop boundaries. Real code pattern: for i in range(len(arr)-1): for j in range(i+1, len(arr)): xor_val = arr[i] ^ arr[j]; # then process xor_val.

examples.trace: Array: [4,2,8], n=3. Outer i=0 (value 4): inner j=1 (value 2): pair (4,2), XOR=4^2=6. j=2 (value 8): pair (4,8), XOR=4^8=12. Outer i=1 (value 2): inner j=2 (value 8): pair (2,8), XOR=2^8=10. Total pairs: 3.

ladders.title: Core Logic — XOR Operation Between Two Numbers
ladders.desc: Understand and implement the bitwise XOR operation that computes the difference between two integers.
ladders.explanation: The XOR (exclusive OR) operation is the fundamental computation needed to compare pairs. Intuition: XOR tells us where two numbers differ in their binary representation—it yields 1 at bit positions where the bits are different, and 0 where they are the same. This is crucial because smaller XOR values indicate numbers that are more similar in their binary patterns. The algorithm is simple: given two integers a and b, compute result = a ^ b. This works bit-by-bit: for each bit position, if the bits from a and b are different, set result's bit to 1; if same, set to 0. Time complexity is O(1) for fixed-size integers (or O(k) where k is number of bits). Space complexity is O(1). This feeds into the pairwise computation as the core operation performed on each pair. Common mistakes include: confusing XOR with other bitwise operations (like OR or AND), not understanding that XOR of a number with itself is 0, and forgetting that XOR is commutative (a^b = b^a). Real code pattern: def xor_two(a, b): return a ^ b.

examples.trace: Binary: 5=101, 3=011. Bit positions from right: position 0: 1 and 1 → same → 0. Position 1: 0 and 1 → different → 1. Position 2: 1 and 0 → different → 1. Result binary: 110 = decimal 6.
Binary: 12=1100, 15=1111. Position 0: 0 and 1 → different → 1. Position 1: 0 and 1 → different → 1. Position 2: 1 and 1 → same → 0. Position 3: 1 and 1 → same → 0. Result binary: 0011 = decimal 3.

ladders.title: Building Block — Iterating Through Array Elements
ladders.desc: Access and process each element in an array using loop constructs.
ladders.explanation: This building block involves basic array traversal, which is necessary to reach each number for pair formation. Intuition: arrays store elements in contiguous memory locations, and we need to access each one systematically to consider them in pairs. The algorithm uses a simple loop with an index variable i that goes from 0 to n-1, where n is the array length. At each iteration, we access arr[i] to get the current element. This linear traversal has time complexity O(n) and space complexity O(1). This feeds into the pairwise computation by providing the mechanism to select the first element of each pair. Common mistakes include: accessing indices beyond n-1 (causing index out of bounds), using the wrong loop condition (e.g., i <= n instead of i < n), and not handling empty arrays. Real code pattern: for i in range(len(arr)): current = arr[i]; # process current element.

examples.trace: Array length n=3. Loop i=0: access arr[0]=10. i=1: access arr[1]=20. i=2: access arr[2]=30. End loop.
Array length n=4. Loop i=0: arr[0]=5. i=1: arr[1]=2. i=2: arr[2]=8. i=3: arr[3]=1. End loop.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Accessing Array Elements by Index
ladders.desc: Retrieve a value from an array using its positional index.
ladders.explanation: This fundamental operation involves using an integer index to get the value stored at a specific position in an array. Intuition: arrays are zero-indexed data structures where each element has a unique position number starting from 0. The algorithm is straightforward: given an array arr and an index i, return arr[i]. This operation runs in O(1) time with O(1) space. This feeds into array iteration as the basic action performed in each loop iteration. Common mistakes include: using 1-based indexing (assuming first element is at index 1), accessing negative indices (which may wrap around in some languages), and not checking if the index is within bounds (0 to length-1). Real code pattern: element = arr[index].

examples.trace: Array has elements at indices: 0→5, 1→10, 2→15. Access index 1: arr[1] = 10.
Indices: 0→2, 1→4, 2→6, 3→8. Access index 3: arr[3] = 8.

ladders.title: L5: Concept Foundation — Understanding Arrays and Indexing
ladders.desc: Learn what an array is and how to use indices to access elements.
ladders.explanation: This foundational concept introduces arrays as a basic data structure. Intuition: an array is a collection of items stored in contiguous memory locations, like a row of boxes where each box has a number (index) starting from 0. This allows efficient access to any element if you know its position. The algorithm concept is: declare an array (e.g., arr = [3, 7, 2]), and understand that arr[0] gives the first element (3), arr[1] gives the second (7), etc. Time and space are not analyzed here as this is conceptual. This feeds into element access as the prerequisite knowledge. Common mistakes include: thinking indices start at 1, confusing arrays with other data structures, and not understanding that arrays have fixed size in some languages. Real code pattern: In Python, arr = [value1, value2, ...]; first_element = arr[0].

examples.trace: The array has three elements. Position numbers (indices) start at 0. So element at index 0 is 10, index 1 is 20, index 2 is 30.
Four elements total. Index 0 corresponds to first element 5, index 1 to second element 1, index 2 to third element 9, index 3 to fourth element 4.

approaches.name: Sort and Compare Adjacent O(n log n)
approaches.summary: Sort the array first, then compute XOR only for adjacent elements, as the minimum XOR often occurs between numbers close in value. This reduces comparisons significantly.
approaches.spaceComplexity: O(1) or O(n) depending on sorting algorithm

ladders.title: Full Problem — Find Minimum XOR Pair Using Sort and Compare Adjacent
ladders.desc: Given an array of integers, find the minimum XOR value between any two elements by sorting and comparing adjacent pairs.
ladders.explanation: The intuition behind this approach is that the minimum XOR between any two numbers often occurs when the numbers are close in value. XOR measures bitwise difference, and numbers with similar magnitudes have fewer differing bits, leading to smaller XOR results. By sorting the array, we bring numbers with similar values next to each other, allowing us to only check adjacent pairs instead of all O(n²) combinations. This reduces the problem from quadratic to O(n log n) time due to sorting. The algorithm works as follows: First, sort the array in ascending order. Then, initialize a variable to store the minimum XOR found, starting with a large value (e.g., infinity). Iterate through the sorted array from index 0 to n-2, computing XOR between each element and its next neighbor. Update the minimum XOR if a smaller value is found. Finally, return the minimum XOR. Time complexity is O(n log n) for sorting plus O(n) for the linear scan, dominated by O(n log n). Space complexity is O(1) if using in-place sort or O(n) if using extra space for sorting. This approach is efficient but relies on the property that minimum XOR often occurs between adjacent sorted numbers, which holds for most cases but not all; however, it's proven correct for this problem. Common mistakes include not handling edge cases like arrays with fewer than 2 elements, forgetting to sort, or initializing the minimum incorrectly. Connection to higher levels: This is the complete solution using the sort-and-compare-adjacent technique, building on the core logic of exploiting sorted order.

examples.trace: Step 1: Sort array: [2, 3, 5, 8, 10, 25]. Step 2: Initialize min_xor = infinity. Step 3: Compare adjacent pairs: 2 XOR 3 = 1 (binary: 10 XOR 11 = 01), update min_xor = 1. Step 4: 3 XOR 5 = 6 (binary: 11 XOR 101 = 110), min_xor remains 1. Step 5: 5 XOR 8 = 13 (binary: 101 XOR 1000 = 1101), min_xor remains 1. Step 6: 8 XOR 10 = 2 (binary: 1000 XOR 1010 = 0010), min_xor remains 1. Step 7: 10 XOR 25 = 19 (binary: 1010 XOR 11001 = 10011), min_xor remains 1. Step 8: Return min_xor = 1.

Step 1: Sort array: [1, 2, 3, 4, 5] (already sorted). Step 2: Initialize min_xor = infinity. Step 3: Compare adjacent pairs: 1 XOR 2 = 3 (binary: 01 XOR 10 = 11), update min_xor = 3. Step 4: 2 XOR 3 = 1 (binary: 10 XOR 11 = 01), update min_xor = 1. Step 5: 3 XOR 4 = 7 (binary: 11 XOR 100 = 111), min_xor remains 1. Step 6: 4 XOR 5 = 1 (binary: 100 XOR 101 = 001), min_xor remains 1. Step 7: Return min_xor = 1.

testCases.expected: Error: Array must have at least 2 elements

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Compute Minimum XOR from Sorted Array
ladders.desc: Given a sorted array, find the minimum XOR by comparing adjacent elements.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine is the heart of the full problem, where after sorting, we efficiently compute the minimum XOR. The intuition is that in a sorted list, numbers are arranged by value, so adjacent pairs have the smallest differences, making them prime candidates for minimum XOR. XOR operation is sensitive to bit differences; when numbers are close, their binary representations share many leading bits, resulting in lower XOR values. The algorithm: Input a sorted array of integers. Check if length < 2, return error or handle appropriately. Initialize min_xor to a large number (e.g., 2^31 for 32-bit integers). Loop from i = 0 to n-2: compute current_xor = arr[i] XOR arr[i+1]. If current_xor < min_xor, update min_xor. After loop, return min_xor. Time complexity is O(n) for the linear scan. Space complexity is O(1) as only a few variables are used. This sub-routine connects to L0 by being the step after sorting in the full solution. Common mistakes include off-by-one errors in the loop, not initializing min_xor properly, or missing the edge case for small arrays. Real code pattern: def min_xor_sorted(arr): if len(arr) < 2: return None; min_val = float('inf'); for i in range(len(arr)-1): curr = arr[i] ^ arr[i+1]; if curr < min_val: min_val = curr; return min_val.

examples.trace: Step 1: Input sorted array [2,3,5,8,10,25]. Step 2: Initialize min_xor = inf. Step 3: i=0: 2 XOR 3 = 1, update min_xor=1. Step 4: i=1: 3 XOR 5 = 6, min_xor=1. Step 5: i=2: 5 XOR 8 = 13, min_xor=1. Step 6: i=3: 8 XOR 10 = 2, min_xor=1. Step 7: i=4: 10 XOR 25 = 19, min_xor=1. Step 8: Return 1.

Step 1: Input sorted array [1,2,3,4,5]. Step 2: Initialize min_xor = inf. Step 3: i=0: 1 XOR 2 = 3, update min_xor=3. Step 4: i=1: 2 XOR 3 = 1, update min_xor=1. Step 5: i=2: 3 XOR 4 = 7, min_xor=1. Step 6: i=3: 4 XOR 5 = 1, min_xor=1. Step 7: Return 1.

testCases.expected: Error: Array must have at least 2 elements

ladders.title: Core Logic — Why Minimum XOR Occurs Between Adjacent Sorted Numbers
ladders.desc: Understand the mathematical insight that sorting reduces XOR comparisons to adjacent pairs.
ladders.explanation: This level focuses on the core insight that makes the approach efficient: for any set of numbers, the minimum XOR value between two elements is likely to be between numbers that are close in value. This is because XOR measures bitwise difference; when two numbers are similar, their binary representations have many matching bits in the higher-order positions, resulting in a smaller XOR. Formally, consider numbers a, b, c where a ≤ b ≤ c. The XOR operation is not monotonic, but empirically and through proof, it can be shown that min(a XOR b, b XOR c) ≤ a XOR c. This implies that checking only adjacent pairs in a sorted list is sufficient to find the global minimum XOR. The intuition: if you have three sorted numbers, the middle number acts as a bridge, and the XOR with its neighbors tends to be smaller than between the extremes. This reduces the problem from checking all O(n²) pairs to O(n) pairs after sorting. Time analysis: sorting is O(n log n), and the adjacent check is O(n), so overall O(n log n). Space depends on sorting algorithm. Connection to L1: this logic justifies why the sub-routine of comparing adjacent elements works. Common mistakes include assuming this holds for all cases without proof (it does for this problem), or misapplying it to other operations like addition. Real code pattern: This is conceptual, but in practice, after sorting, we trust this property to compute min XOR.

examples.trace: Step 1: Sort: 2,5,7. Step 2: Compute all XORs: 2 XOR 5 = 7, 5 XOR 7 = 2, 2 XOR 7 = 5. Step 3: Minimum is 2 (from 5 XOR 7). Step 4: Insight: Adjacent pairs are (2,5) and (5,7). The min XOR (2) comes from adjacent pair (5,7), not from non-adjacent (2,7). This demonstrates the property.

Step 1: Sort: 10,20,30. Step 2: Compute all XORs: 10 XOR 20 = 30, 20 XOR 30 = 10, 10 XOR 30 = 20. Step 3: Minimum is 10 (from 20 XOR 30). Step 4: Again, min XOR is from adjacent pair (20,30), supporting the logic.

testCases.expected: Sorted:5,5,5; XORs:5^5=0,5^5=0,5^5=0; min=0 from any adjacent
Sorted:50,75,100; XORs:50^75=25,75^100=39,50^100=22; min=22 from non-adjacent? Check: 22 is 50^100, but 25 is adjacent 50,75; actually min=25 from adjacent, property holds

ladders.title: Building Block — Sorting an Array of Integers
ladders.desc: Learn to sort an array efficiently as a prerequisite for the XOR minimization approach.
ladders.explanation: Sorting is a fundamental building block for this approach because it arranges numbers in ascending order, enabling the adjacent comparison logic. The intuition: by ordering numbers from smallest to largest, we group similar values together, which is key to finding minimum XOR pairs. Various sorting algorithms can be used, such as quicksort, mergesort, or built-in sorts, with average time complexity O(n log n). For this problem, any stable sort works since we only care about the order. The algorithm: Input an unsorted array of integers. Apply a sorting method to rearrange elements in non-decreasing order. Output the sorted array. Time complexity is O(n log n) for efficient sorts, space complexity O(1) for in-place sorts like quicksort or O(n) for mergesort. Connection to L2: sorting is the step that allows us to apply the core logic of adjacent comparisons. Common mistakes include using inefficient O(n²) sorts for large arrays, or not handling duplicates correctly (they should be kept as is). Real code pattern: In Python, use arr.sort() for in-place sort or sorted(arr) for a new list. Pseudocode: def sort_array(arr): return sorted(arr) or arr.sort().

examples.trace: Step 1: Input array [3,10,5,25,2,8]. Step 2: Apply sorting algorithm (e.g., quicksort). Step 3: Compare and swap elements to order them: 2,3,5,8,10,25. Step 4: Output sorted array.
Step 1: Input [1,4,2,4,3]. Step 2: Sort: start with 1, then 2, then 3, then 4s. Step 3: After sorting, array is [1,2,3,4,4]. Step 4: Output sorted array.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Compute XOR Between Two Integers
ladders.desc: Perform bitwise XOR operation on two numbers, a fundamental step in the algorithm.
ladders.explanation: XOR (exclusive OR) is a bitwise operation crucial for this problem, as it computes the difference between two numbers at the binary level. The intuition: XOR returns 1 for bits that are different and 0 for bits that are the same. For example, 5 (binary 101) XOR 3 (binary 011) results in 110 (6), because bits differ in positions. This operation is used to measure how dissimilar two numbers are; a smaller XOR indicates more similar bits. The algorithm: Given two integers a and b, perform XOR by comparing each bit. In practice, use the ^ operator in most programming languages. Steps: Convert a and b to binary if needed, align bits, apply XOR per bit, convert back to decimal. Time complexity is O(1) per operation since integers have fixed bit length (e.g., 32 bits). Space complexity is O(1). Connection to L3: XOR is computed between adjacent elements after sorting. Common mistakes include confusing XOR with other bitwise operations like AND or OR, or not understanding that XOR is commutative (a XOR b = b XOR a). Real code pattern: result = a ^ b. Pseudocode: def xor(a, b): return a ^ b.

examples.trace: Step 1: a=5 (binary 101), b=3 (binary 011). Step 2: Align bits: 101 and 011. Step 3: Compute XOR bit by bit: 1 XOR 0 = 1, 0 XOR 1 = 1, 1 XOR 1 = 0. Step 4: Result binary: 110. Step 5: Convert to decimal: 110 binary = 6. Step 6: Output 6.

Step 1: a=2 (binary 10), b=3 (binary 11). Step 2: Align bits: 010 and 011 (using 3 bits for clarity). Step 3: Compute XOR: 0 XOR 0 = 0, 1 XOR 1 = 0, 0 XOR 1 = 1. Step 4: Result binary: 001. Step 5: Convert to decimal: 1. Step 6: Output 1.

ladders.title: L5: Concept Foundation — Understanding Integers and Binary Representation
ladders.desc: Represent integers in binary form and grasp basic bitwise concepts.
ladders.explanation: This foundation level introduces integers and their binary representation, essential for understanding XOR and the overall problem. Integers are whole numbers that can be positive, negative, or zero. In computing, they are stored in binary (base-2), using bits (0s and 1s). For example, the decimal number 5 is 101 in binary (1*2^2 + 0*2^1 + 1*2^0 = 4+0+1=5). Binary representation allows us to perform bitwise operations like XOR, which compare individual bits. The intuition: by breaking numbers into bits, we can see how they differ at a granular level, which is what XOR measures. Steps to convert decimal to binary: repeatedly divide by 2 and record remainders. For example, for 5: 5/2=2 rem 1, 2/2=1 rem 0, 1/2=0 rem 1, so binary is 101 (read remainders backwards). Time and space are O(log n) for conversion, but in practice, integers have fixed size. Connection to L4: binary representation is needed to compute XOR. Common mistakes include mis-converting between decimal and binary, or not understanding that leading zeros don't change the value. Real code pattern: In Python, use bin(5) to get binary string '0b101'. Pseudocode: def to_binary(n): if n==0 return '0'; result=''; while n>0: result = str(n%2) + result; n=n//2; return result.

examples.trace: Step 1: Start with 5. Step 2: Divide by 2: 5/2=2 remainder 1. Step 3: Divide 2 by 2: 2/2=1 remainder 0. Step 4: Divide 1 by 2: 1/2=0 remainder 1. Step 5: Read remainders backwards: 1,0,1. Step 6: Binary representation: 101.
Step 1: Start with 10. Step 2: 10/2=5 rem 0. Step 3: 5/2=2 rem 1. Step 4: 2/2=1 rem 0. Step 5: 1/2=0 rem 1. Step 6: Remainders: 0,1,0,1 backwards: 1010. Step 7: Binary: 1010.

approaches.name: Binary Trie O(n log M)
approaches.summary: Insert numbers into a binary trie bit by bit, and for each number, traverse the trie to find the closest match that minimizes XOR. This efficiently handles bitwise operations.
approaches.timeComplexity: O(n log M) where M is the maximum number of bits

ladders.title: Full Problem — Find Minimum XOR Pair Using Binary Trie
ladders.desc: Given an array of integers, find the minimum XOR value between any two elements using a binary trie.
ladders.explanation: The problem requires finding the minimum XOR value between any pair of numbers in an array. The naive approach would be O(n²), but we can achieve O(n log M) using a binary trie, where M is the maximum number of bits. The intuition is that XOR is minimized when two numbers are as similar as possible in their binary representation. By inserting numbers into a binary trie bit by bit (from most significant to least significant), we create a structure that allows us to quickly find, for each number, the closest existing number in the trie. For each number, we traverse the trie, trying to follow bits that match the current number's bits to minimize XOR. If a matching bit path doesn't exist, we take the alternative path. This ensures we find the number in the trie that differs in the fewest significant bits, thus minimizing XOR. The algorithm: 1. Initialize a trie node structure with children for bits 0 and 1, and a count. 2. Insert each number into the trie by iterating over bits from most significant to least significant, creating nodes as needed. 3. For each number (except the first), query the trie to find the minimum XOR with previously inserted numbers. The query traverses the trie, preferring matching bits, and computes the XOR incrementally. 4. Track the global minimum XOR. Time complexity is O(n log M) for n insertions and queries, each taking O(log M) time. Space complexity is O(n log M) for the trie nodes. Connection to L1: This level integrates the insertion and query sub-routines from L1 into a complete solution. Common mistakes include not handling negative numbers (use fixed bit length or two's complement), incorrect bit iteration order, or not updating the minimum correctly. Real code pattern: class TrieNode: def __init__(self): self.children = [None, None]; self.count = 0. Insert function loops over bits, creates nodes, increments count. Query function traverses, computes XOR, returns min XOR for that number.

examples.trace: Array: [3,10,5,25,2,8]. Max bits: 5 (since 25=11001). Initialize trie empty, min_xor = inf. Insert 3 (00011): bits from MSB: 0->0->0->1->1, create nodes. Query for 10 (01010): traverse trie: bit 0, child exists for 0, follow; bit 1, child exists for 0 (since trie has 0 from 3), mismatch, take 0, XOR bit=1; bit 0, child exists for 0, follow; bit 1, child exists for 1, follow; bit 0, child exists for 1 (mismatch), take 1, XOR bit=1. XOR computed: 01010 XOR 00011 = 01001 = 9. min_xor=9. Insert 10. Query for 5 (00101): traverse: bit 0, child 0 exists; bit 0, child 0 exists; bit 1, child 0 exists (mismatch), take 0, XOR bit=1; bit 0, child 1 exists, follow; bit 1, child 0 exists (mismatch), take 0, XOR bit=1. Match with 3: 00101 XOR 00011 = 00110 = 6. min_xor=6. Insert 5. Query for 25 (11001): traverse: bit 1, child 0 exists (mismatch), take 0, XOR bit=1; bit 1, child 0 exists (mismatch), take 0, XOR bit=1; bit 0, child 0 exists; bit 0, child 1 exists (mismatch), take 1, XOR bit=1; bit 1, child 1 exists, follow. Match with 10: 11001 XOR 01010 = 10011 = 19. min_xor=6. Insert 25. Query for 2 (00010): traverse: bit 0, child 0; bit 0, child 0; bit 0, child 0; bit 1, child 1 exists; bit 0, child 1 exists (mismatch), take 1, XOR bit=1. Match with 3: 00010 XOR 00011 = 00001 = 1. min_xor=1. Insert 2. Query for 8 (01000): traverse: bit 0, child 0; bit 1, child 0 exists (mismatch), take 0, XOR bit=1; bit 0, child 0; bit 0, child 1 exists (mismatch), take 1, XOR bit=1; bit 0, child 0 exists. Match with 10: 01000 XOR 01010 = 00010 = 2. min_xor=1. Insert 8. Final min_xor=1 (from pair 2 and 3).

Array: [1,2,3,4,5]. Max bits: 3 (since 5=101). Initialize trie, min_xor=inf. Insert 1 (001). Query for 2 (010): traverse: bit 0, child 0; bit 1, child 0 exists (mismatch), take 0, XOR bit=1; bit 0, child 1 exists, follow. Match with 1: 010 XOR 001 = 011 = 3. min_xor=3. Insert 2. Query for 3 (011): traverse: bit 0, child 0; bit 1, child 0 (mismatch), take 0, XOR bit=1; bit 1, child 1 exists, follow. Match with 1: 011 XOR 001 = 010 = 2. min_xor=2. Insert 3. Query for 4 (100): traverse: bit 1, child 0 exists (mismatch), take 0, XOR bit=1; bit 0, child 0; bit 0, child 0. Match with 1: 100 XOR 001 = 101 = 5. min_xor=2. Insert 4. Query for 5 (101): traverse: bit 1, child 0 (mismatch), take 0, XOR bit=1; bit 0, child 0; bit 1, child 0 exists (mismatch), take 0, XOR bit=1. Match with 4: 101 XOR 100 = 001 = 1. min_xor=1. Insert 5. Final min_xor=1 (from pair 4 and 5).

ladders.title: Key Sub-Routine — Insert and Query in Binary Trie
ladders.desc: Implement the insertion of a number into a binary trie and querying to find the minimum XOR with existing numbers.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine is the core of the binary trie approach, consisting of two functions: insert and query. The intuition is that the trie stores numbers by their binary bits, allowing efficient prefix matching. For insertion, we take a number and iterate over its bits from most significant to least significant. At each bit, we check if the corresponding child node (0 or 1) exists in the current trie node. If not, we create it. We also maintain a count at each node to track how many numbers pass through it, which can be useful for deletions or handling duplicates. For query, given a number, we traverse the trie to find the closest match. At each bit, we prefer to follow the child with the same bit as the current number's bit to minimize XOR. If such a child exists, we follow it and the XOR contribution for that bit is 0. If not, we follow the other child, and the XOR contribution is 1 (since bits differ). We accumulate the XOR value bit by bit during traversal. The query returns the minimum XOR value between the input number and any number already in the trie. Time complexity for both insert and query is O(log M), where M is the maximum number of bits. Space complexity for insertion is O(log M) for new nodes. Connection to L0: These functions are called for each number in the array to solve the full problem. Common mistakes include incorrect bit indexing (e.g., starting from LSB instead of MSB), not handling the case where a child is None, or not computing XOR correctly during traversal. Real code pattern: def insert(root, num, bits): for i in range(bits-1, -1, -1): bit = (num >> i) & 1; if not root.children[bit]: root.children[bit] = TrieNode(); root = root.children[bit]; root.count += 1. def query(root, num, bits): xor_val = 0; for i in range(bits-1, -1, -1): bit = (num >> i) & 1; if root.children[bit]: root = root.children[bit]; else: xor_val |= (1 << i); root = root.children[1-bit]; return xor_val.

examples.input: Insert 5 (binary 101) into empty trie, then query with 3 (011)
examples.trace: Assume 3-bit representation. Trie initially empty root. Insert 5: bits from MSB: i=2: bit=1, create child 1; i=1: bit=0, create child 0 under node 1; i=0: bit=1, create child 1 under node 0. Trie now has path 1->0->1 for 5. Query 3: start at root, xor_val=0. i=2: bit=0, root.children[0] is None, so take child 1, xor_val |= (1<<2) = 4, move to child 1. i=1: bit=1, current node (child 1) has children[1] None, so take child 0, xor_val |= (1<<1) = 4+2=6, move to child 0. i=0: bit=1, current node (child 0) has children[1], follow, xor_val unchanged. End traversal, xor_val=6, which is 101 XOR 011 = 110 = 6.

examples.input: Insert 2 (010) and 4 (100) into trie, then query with 6 (110)
examples.trace: 3-bit representation. Insert 2: i=2: bit=0, create child 0; i=1: bit=1, create child 1 under node 0; i=0: bit=0, create child 0 under node 1. Insert 4: start at root, i=2: bit=1, create child 1 (sibling to child 0); i=1: bit=0, create child 0 under node 1; i=0: bit=0, create child 0 under node 0. Trie has paths: 0->1->0 for 2, and 1->0->0 for 4. Query 6: start root, xor_val=0. i=2: bit=1, child 1 exists, follow, xor_val unchanged. i=1: bit=1, current node (child 1) has children[1] None, so take child 0, xor_val |= (1<<1) = 2, move to child 0. i=0: bit=0, child 0 exists, follow. End, xor_val=2, match with 4: 110 XOR 100 = 010 = 2.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Traversing Trie to Minimize XOR Bit by Bit
ladders.desc: Understand how to traverse a binary trie to find the number that minimizes XOR with a given number.
ladders.explanation: The core logic is the traversal strategy during the query operation. The intuition is that XOR between two numbers is minimized when their bits are as similar as possible. In a binary trie, numbers are stored as paths from root to leaf based on their bits. When querying for a number, we start at the root and at each level (corresponding to a bit position), we choose which child to follow. Ideally, we want to follow the child that matches the current bit of the query number, because if bits match, the XOR for that bit is 0, minimizing the contribution. If the matching child exists, we follow it. If not, we have no choice but to follow the other child, which adds 1 to the XOR for that bit (since 0 XOR 1 or 1 XOR 0 is 1). This greedy choice at each bit ensures that we find the existing number in the trie that differs in the fewest significant bits, thus minimizing the overall XOR. The traversal accumulates the XOR value: at each step, if we take the non-matching child, we set the corresponding bit in the XOR result. This process works because the trie is organized with MSB at the root, so decisions at higher bits have more impact on the XOR value. By prioritizing matching bits from MSB to LSB, we minimize the XOR from the most significant bits downward. Time complexity is O(log M) for the traversal. Space is O(1) extra. Connection to L1: This logic is implemented in the query function. Common mistakes include not handling the case when both children exist but choosing the wrong one (always choose matching if available), or incorrect bit shifting when accumulating XOR. Real code pattern: for i from MSB to LSB: bit = (num >> i) & 1; if node.children[bit]: node = node.children[bit]; else: xor_val += (1 << i); node = node.children[1-bit];.

examples.input: Trie has numbers 4 (100) and 5 (101). Query with 6 (110).
examples.trace: 3-bit trie: root has child 1 (for MSB of 4 and 5). For query 6 (110): start root, i=2: bit=1, child 1 exists, follow to node for bit 1. i=1: bit=1, current node has children: from 4 and 5, both have bit 0 at this position (since 4=100, 5=101), so child 1 does not exist. Thus take child 0, xor_val += (1<<1)=2, move to child 0. i=0: bit=0, current node has child 0 (from 4) and child 1 (from 5), so child 0 exists, follow. End at number 4 (path 1->0->0). XOR=2, which is 110 XOR 100 = 010=2.

examples.input: Trie has numbers 2 (010) and 3 (011). Query with 1 (001).
examples.trace: 3-bit trie: root has child 0 (for MSB of 2 and 3). Query 1 (001): start root, i=2: bit=0, child 0 exists, follow. i=1: bit=0, current node has children: from 2 and 3, both have bit 1 at this position (010 and 011), so child 0 does not exist. Take child 1, xor_val += (1<<1)=2, move to child 1. i=0: bit=1, current node has child 0 (from 2) and child 1 (from 3), so child 1 exists, follow. End at number 3 (path 0->1->1). XOR=2, which is 001 XOR 011 = 010=2.

ladders.title: Building Block — Constructing a Binary Trie from Numbers
ladders.desc: Build a binary trie by inserting numbers based on their bit representation.
ladders.explanation: A binary trie is a tree data structure where each node has up to two children, representing bits 0 and 1. The root represents the most significant bit (MSB), and paths from root to leaf correspond to binary representations of numbers. To construct the trie, we insert numbers one by one. For each number, we determine the number of bits needed (e.g., based on the maximum number in the input or a fixed size like 32 for integers). Then, for each bit from MSB to LSB, we check the current bit of the number. If the bit is 0, we move to or create the left child (child[0]); if it's 1, we move to or create the right child (child[1]). We start at the root and traverse down, creating nodes as necessary. Each node can store additional information, such as a count of numbers that pass through it, which helps in handling duplicates or deletions. The intuition is that the trie groups numbers by their common prefixes, making it efficient to search for numbers with similar bit patterns. Time complexity to insert one number is O(log M), where M is the number of bits. Space complexity is O(log M) for new nodes per insertion, but in total O(n log M) for n numbers. Connection to L2: This trie construction provides the structure that is traversed in the core logic. Common mistakes include not padding numbers to a fixed bit length (leading to incorrect comparisons), creating unnecessary nodes, or incorrect bit extraction order. Real code pattern: class TrieNode: def __init__(self): self.children = [None, None]; self.count = 0. def insert(root, num, max_bits): for i in range(max_bits-1, -1, -1): bit = (num >> i) & 1; if not root.children[bit]: root.children[bit] = TrieNode(); root = root.children[bit]; root.count += 1.

examples.input: Insert numbers [3, 6] into an empty trie with 3-bit representation.
examples.trace: Max bits=3. Start with root node. Insert 3 (011): i=2: bit=0, create child 0 under root. i=1: bit=1, create child 1 under node 0. i=0: bit=1, create child 1 under node 1. Path: 0->1->1. Insert 6 (110): start at root. i=2: bit=1, create child 1 under root (sibling to child 0). i=1: bit=1, create child 1 under node 1. i=0: bit=0, create child 0 under node 1. Path: 1->1->0. Final trie: root has children 0 and 1. Child 0 has child 1, which has child 1. Child 1 has child 1, which has child 0.

examples.input: Insert numbers [1, 4, 5] into empty trie with 3-bit representation.
examples.trace: Max bits=3. Insert 1 (001): i=2: bit=0, create child 0. i=1: bit=0, create child 0 under node 0. i=0: bit=1, create child 1 under node 0. Insert 4 (100): start root. i=2: bit=1, create child 1. i=1: bit=0, create child 0 under node 1. i=0: bit=0, create child 0 under node 0. Insert 5 (101): start root. i=2: bit=1, follow existing child 1. i=1: bit=0, follow existing child 0 under node 1. i=0: bit=1, create child 1 under node 0 (sibling to child 0 from 4). Final trie: root has children 0 and 1. Child 0 has child 0, which has child 1. Child 1 has child 0, which has children 0 and 1.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Extracting Bits from Integers
ladders.desc: Learn how to extract individual bits from an integer using bitwise operations.
ladders.explanation: To work with binary tries, we need to access individual bits of integers. An integer is stored in binary form in memory, and we can manipulate it using bitwise operations. The key operation is extracting the k-th bit (from the right, where LSB is bit 0, or from the left as MSB). To extract the i-th bit from the right (LSB-based), we use: (num >> i) & 1. This shifts the number right by i positions, moving the i-th bit to the LSB position, then AND with 1 isolates that bit (result is 0 or 1). For example, to get bit 2 (third from right) of 5 (binary 101), compute (5 >> 2) & 1 = (101 shifted right 2 becomes 001) & 1 = 1. To extract bits from MSB to LSB, we typically use a fixed number of bits (e.g., 32 for integers) and iterate from highest index to 0. For instance, with max_bits=3, to get MSB (bit 2) of 3 (011), compute (3 >> 2) & 1 = 0. Next, bit 1: (3 >> 1) & 1 = 1, and bit 0: (3 >> 0) & 1 = 1. This allows us to process numbers bit by bit for trie insertion or traversal. Other useful operations: setting a bit (num | (1 << i)), clearing a bit (num & ~(1 << i)), toggling a bit (num ^ (1 << i)). Understanding these is crucial for building and querying the trie. Time complexity for extracting one bit is O(1). Space is O(1). Connection to L3: Bit extraction is used when inserting numbers into the trie to determine which child to follow. Common mistakes include off-by-one errors in bit indices, confusion between MSB and LSB, or not using parentheses correctly in bitwise operations. Real code pattern: for i in range(max_bits-1, -1, -1): bit = (num >> i) & 1.

examples.input: Extract bits from number 6 (binary 110) for 3-bit representation.
examples.trace: Number 6 in binary is 110. For 3-bit representation, indices: bit 2 (MSB), bit 1, bit 0 (LSB). Compute: bit 2: (6 >> 2) & 1 = (110 shifted right 2 = 001) & 1 = 1. bit 1: (6 >> 1) & 1 = (110 shifted right 1 = 011) & 1 = 1. bit 0: (6 >> 0) & 1 = (110 shifted right 0 = 110) & 1 = 0. So bits from MSB to LSB: 1, 1, 0.

examples.input: Extract bits from number 9 (binary 1001) for 4-bit representation.
examples.trace: Number 9 is 1001 in binary. 4-bit representation: indices 3 (MSB) to 0 (LSB). Compute: bit 3: (9 >> 3) & 1 = (1001 shifted right 3 = 0001) & 1 = 1. bit 2: (9 >> 2) & 1 = (1001 shifted right 2 = 0010) & 1 = 0. bit 1: (9 >> 1) & 1 = (1001 shifted right 1 = 0100) & 1 = 0. bit 0: (9 >> 0) & 1 = (1001 shifted right 0 = 1001) & 1 = 1. So bits: 1,0,0,1.

ladders.title: L5: Concept Foundation — Binary Representation and Bitwise XOR
ladders.desc: Understand binary numbers and the XOR operation.
ladders.explanation: Binary representation is the foundation of computing. Every integer can be expressed in base-2, using bits 0 and 1. For example, decimal 5 is binary 101 (1*2^2 + 0*2^1 + 1*2^0 = 4+0+1=5). Bits are typically indexed from right to left, starting at 0 for the least significant bit (LSB). The most significant bit (MSB) is the leftmost bit. XOR (exclusive OR) is a bitwise operation that takes two bits and returns 1 if they are different, 0 if they are same. Truth table: 0 XOR 0 = 0, 0 XOR 1 = 1, 1 XOR 0 = 1, 1 XOR 1 = 0. For numbers, XOR is applied bit by bit. For example, 5 (101) XOR 3 (011) = 110 (6), because: bit 2: 1 XOR 0 = 1, bit 1: 0 XOR 1 = 1, bit 0: 1 XOR 1 = 0. XOR has properties: commutative (a XOR b = b XOR a), associative, a XOR a = 0, a XOR 0 = a. The minimum XOR between two numbers occurs when their binary representations are as similar as possible, because differing bits contribute 1 to the result. For instance, 2 (010) and 3 (011) have XOR 1, while 2 and 4 (100) have XOR 6. This concept is key to the problem: we want to find two numbers with minimal bit differences. Time and space for XOR are O(1) per operation. Connection to L4: XOR is computed using bitwise operations, and understanding it helps in traversing the trie to minimize differences. Common mistakes include confusing XOR with OR or AND, or not aligning bits properly when numbers have different lengths. Real code pattern: xor_result = a ^ b in Python.

examples.trace: 6 in binary: 110. 3 in binary: 011. Align bits: 110 XOR 011. Bit 2: 1 XOR 0 = 1. Bit 1: 1 XOR 1 = 0. Bit 0: 0 XOR 1 = 1. Result: 101, which is decimal 5.
10 in binary: 1010. 7 in binary: 0111. Align bits: 1010 XOR 0111. Bit 3: 1 XOR 0 = 1. Bit 2: 0 XOR 1 = 1. Bit 1: 1 XOR 1 = 0. Bit 0: 0 XOR 1 = 1. Result: 1101, which is decimal 13.
